title: Collision Dynamics of False-Vacuum Oscillons
authors: J. G. F. Campos, N. S. Manton, Azadeh Mohammadi
abstract: We study the collision dynamics of localized oscillons in two classes of $(1+1)$-dimensional scalar field theories with metastable false vacua, a normal class with a positive quartic self-interaction term and an inverted class with a negative quartic term. We construct small-amplitude oscillon solutions around the false vacuum using the Fodor {\emph{et al.}} expansion, and show that the force between oscillons decays exponentially at large separation, with a strength modulated by their relative phase. Numerical simulations of two-oscillon collisions exhibit reflection, crossing, and formation of excited oscillons. Resonance windows occur, similar to those found in kink-antikink collisions. In the normal theory, if the oscillons have sufficient energy, the field can pass over a sphaleron barrier and evolve into a kink-antikink pair, initiating a phase transition to the true vacuum. We also simulate the collision of oscillons evolved from a slightly perturbed sphaleron.
summary: This paper explores how the collision of stable, oscillating field configurations called oscillons can trigger a phase transition in a metastable vacuum. It demonstrates that sufficiently energetic collisions can overcome a potential barrier to create new field structures, effectively initiating vacuum decay.
main_problem: The study investigates the collision dynamics of localized oscillons in (1+1)-dimensional scalar field theories and determines if these collisions can trigger a phase transition from a false vacuum to a true vacuum.
main_result: Oscillon collisions exhibit complex outcomes including reflection, crossing, and merging, with high-energy collisions capable of crossing the sphaleron barrier to create kink-antikink pairs and initiate vacuum decay. The inter-oscillon force decays exponentially with separation and is modulated by their relative phase.
method: The authors use the Fodor et al. expansion for small-amplitude analytical solutions and perform numerical simulations using a Fourier spectral method for spatial derivatives and a fourth-order Runge-Kutta integrator.
model_or_system: The system consists of (1+1)-dimensional scalar field theories featuring two classes of potentials: a 'normal' class with a positive quartic self-interaction and an 'inverted' class with a negative quartic term obtained via analytic continuation.
key_observables: Oscillon frequency, inter-oscillon force, collision outcomes (reflection, crossing, merging, vacuum decay), power spectrum, and sphaleron mass.
important_parameters: Collision velocity (v), relative phase between oscillons (delta), scaling/size parameter (s), and amplitude/width parameter (epsilon).
main_assumptions: The study relies on the assumption that the long-lived nature of oscillons can be captured via small-amplitude expansions and numerical integration, though the exact mathematical nature of oscillons remains partially understood.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the potential landscapes (normal and inverted), sphaleron profiles and stability potentials, the small oscillation spectrum, and spacetime field evolution showing various collision outcomes and quasi-fractal patterns.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scalar field models, develops analytical solutions for small-amplitude oscillons, presents numerical results for single-oscillon and sphaleron stability, details the dynamics of two-oscillon collisions (including phase and velocity dependence), and concludes with implications for vacuum decay.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
